coredata.dc:title: “Switch-On” fluorescent nanosensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots-MnO2 nanocomposites for probing the activity of acid phosphatase
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Zhenmao
Lin, Xiaoyun
Wu, Lina
Zhao, Chengfei
Zheng, Yanjie
Liu, Ailin
Lin, Liqing
Lin, Xinhua
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a convenient and effective fluorescent “Switch-On” nanosensor was developed for the sensitive and selective detection of acid phosphatase (ACP) activity based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)-manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanocomposites. Due to the formation of N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites, the fluorescence of N-CDs could be greatly quenched by MnO2 nanosheets through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Upon the introduction of L-ascorbic acid (AA), MnO2 nanosheets could be reduced to Mn2+ ions, which resulted in sufficient recovery of fluorescent signal. Thus, the ACP can be monitored based on the enzymatic hydrolysis of l-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) by ACP to generate AA. The quantitative determination of ACP activity in the range of 5–40 U L−1 with the detection limit of 0.1 U L−1 can be achieved in this sensing system. Endowed with high sensitivity and selectivity, the developed nanosensor is capable of monitoring ACP level in human serum samples with satisfactory results, indicating its great potential to be used in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
MnO2 nanosheets
Nanocomposites
Acid phosphatase
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites sensing probe
Fluorescence detection process for ACP activity
Procedure for determining ACP level in human serum
Characterization of N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
Sensing principle of ACP based on N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites
The fluorescence recovery of N-CDs by AA
ACP activity determination based on N-CDs-MnO2 nanosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21775023 and 21705021 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province ( 2015J01595 ), Medical Elite Cultivation Program of Fujian, P.R.C . ( 2014-ZQN-JC-23 ), the Scientific Research Major Program of Fujian Medical University ( JS15004 ), Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province ( 2016Y9055 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the design and principle for ACP detection using N-CDs-MnO2 nanoprobe.

(A) TEM and high-resolution TEM (inset) images of the as-prepared N-CDs. (B) Diameter distribution of N-CDs. (C) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs. (D) The survey XPS spectrum of N-CDs. (E) UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence excitation (λex = 360 nm) and emission (λem = 448 nm) spectra of N-CDs in aqueous solutions. Inset shows the photograph of the N-CDs in aqueous solution under 365 nm UV light. (F) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs using excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm.
(A) UV–vis absorption of MnO2 nanosheets. The inset is a photograph of the MnO2 nanosheets solution taken under visible light. (B) The TEM image of MnO2 nanosheets.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) N-CDs, (b) N-CDs + MnO2, (c) N-CDs + MnO2+ACP, (d) N-CDs + MnO2+AAP and (e) N-CDs + MnO2+AAP + ACP. Inset: corresponding photographs of each solution taken under visible light (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom). (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, MnO2 nanosheets, N-CDs + MnO2 nanocomposites, AAP + ACP reactants and N-CDs + MnO2 nanocomposites containing AAP + ACP reactants.

(A) Fluorescence of N-CDs with the addition of different concentrations of MnO2 nanosheets. Inset: The relationship between quenching efficiency and the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets. (B) Photographs of the N-CDs solution with different concentrations of MnO2 nanosheets taken under visible light (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs-MnO2 nanomaterials upon addition of different concentrations of AA. Inset: Dependence of (F-F0)/F0 on the concentration of AA. F0 and F are fluorescence intensities at 448 nm in the absence and presence of AA, respectively. (B) The linearity relationship between [(F-F0)/F0] and the concentration of AA. The data shown in the figures represent the average of three independent experiments (n = 3).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs-MnO2 nanomaterials in the presence of different units of ACP activity. (B) The linear fitting curve between [(F-F0)/F0] and ACP level. F0 and F are fluorescence intensities without and with ACP, respectively. (C) Specificity of the N-CDs-MnO2 nanoprobe against ACP, Ure, Lys, TRY, GOx and ALP. (D) ACP inhibition efficiency of NaF. The data shown in the figures represent the average of three independent experiments (n = 3).
Measurements of ACP activity in real human serum samples using the proposed nanoprobe.

head.ce:title: “Switch-On” fluorescent nanosensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots-MnO2 nanocomposites for probing the activity of acid phosphatase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350108, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350108, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contributed equally to the present study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a convenient and effective fluorescent “Switch-On” nanosensor was developed for the sensitive and selective detection of acid phosphatase (ACP) activity based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)-manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanocomposites. Due to the formation of N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites, the fluorescence of N-CDs could be greatly quenched by MnO2 nanosheets through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Upon the introduction of L-ascorbic acid (AA), MnO2 nanosheets could be reduced to Mn2+ ions, which resulted in sufficient recovery of fluorescent signal. Thus, the ACP can be monitored based on the enzymatic hydrolysis of l-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) by ACP to generate AA. The quantitative determination of ACP activity in the range of 5–40 U L−1 with the detection limit of 0.1 U L−1 can be achieved in this sensing system. Endowed with high sensitivity and selectivity, the developed nanosensor is capable of monitoring ACP level in human serum samples with satisfactory results, indicating its great potential to be used in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Acid phosphatase (ACP) is a type of hydrolase that can efficiently catalyze the hydrolysis of phosphate esters to liberate free phosphate groups during digestion under weakly acidic environment [1]. It is a kind of essential phosphomonoesterase in nature, which can be found in various mammalian tissues and liquids [2]. Although the concentrations of ACP are found to be normally low in mammalian cells, it plays a critical role in regulating a variety of cellular functions [3]. It has been proved that an abnormally elevated level of ACP in the human body indicates onset of several diseases, such as metastasized prostate cancer [4], hyperparathyroidism [5], Gaucher’s disease [6], multiple myeloma [7] and enzyme disorders related to kidneys, veins, and bones [4]. Therefore, the precisely monitoring of ACP level in physiological media is of great significance for pathologic diagnosis, postsurgical evaluation and drug screening of these relative diseases [8, 9]. Clinically, the measurement of ACP activity has been used to diagnose cancers and monitor cell viability [10]. In fact, ACP has already been recognized as an important tumor marker of metastatic prostate cancer [11–13]. Thus, the establishment of reliable and sensitive ACP assays has important clinical significance.

To date, a number of methods to monitor ACP have been developed, including immunoassay [14], spectrophotometry [1], high performance liquid chromatography [15] and electrochemical methods [16]. Although these methods offer reasonable sensitivities, most of them require tedious purification of samples, complicated instrumental setup and costly bio-molecular reagents. Fluorometric assay is considered as an ideal method because of its convenience, high sensitivity and accessible instrument requirement. However, most of fluorescent assays for ACP detection adopted organic dyes or fluorescent polymers as the fluorogenic indicators [[1, 17, 18]. The applications of these methods are limited due to complicated synthesis procedures, poor stability of organic dyes and lack of sufficient sensitivity for those based on fluorescent polymers [19]. So, it still remains a challenging task to develop a reliable and sensitive ACP assay based on a novel fluorogenic indicator.

Carbon dots (CDs) as rising fluorescent nanomaterials have drawn great attention due to their high aqueous solubility, outstanding optical properties, good chemical stability, tunable surface functionalities, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [20]. Moreover, as a green alternative to toxic transition metal QDs, CDs have displayed outstanding performance in sensors, bioimaging, photocatalysis, optoelectronic devices, drug and gene delivery [19, 21]. Compared with organic dyes or fluorescent polymers, CDs, in particular the heteroatom-doped (such as nitrogen, boron, sulfur atoms) CDs, possess some greater merits including stable luminescence, high quantum yield (QY), good photostability, excellent biocompatibility and more importantly, easy modulation in their intrinsic properties [22]. In recent years, the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) have been used to design new fluorescence chemosensors and biosensors, and showed attractive applications [23–25].

Manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets are a typical kind of two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterial with a broad absorption peak and superior light absorption capability. The absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets has a large overlap with the emission spectrum of many fluorescent materials, which is the indispensable factor for the generation of FRET [26, 27]. In addition, MnO2 nanosheets themselves have strong oxidation capacity and can oxidize some special small reductive biological molecules. Moreover, MnO2 nanosheets can be reduced to Mn2+, which may be friendly to the environment and human health [28]. Thus, the application of MnO2 nanosheets to develop “Switch-On” type fluorescent sensing platforms is very attractive.

Herein, we firstly develop a novel and simple method for the synthesis of N-CDs with high QY by pyrolyzing low-cost folic acid within 8 min. We establish a convenient and effective fluorescent sensing platform for the assay of ACP activity by using N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites as a fluorescent nanoprobe, wherein N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were employed as the energy donor-acceptor pairs. The analytical principle is illustrated in Scheme 1 : The fluorescence of N-CDs was initially quenched effectively by MnO2 nanosheets via the FRET process [29, 30], and then restored by AA produced from the hydrolysis of AAP under the catalysis of ACP. The present fluorescent nanosensor based on N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites for ACP activity detection has not been reported before, and this sensing system exhibits significant advantages including preparation-simple, cost-effective as well as environmental-friendly.
Details on the materials, characterization methods, preparation of normal MnO2 nanosheets and optimization of detection conditions are illustrated in Supplementary Information.

The as-prepared N-CDs were facilely synthesized via a pyrolysis strategy. In short, 0.3 g of folic acid (FA) was added to a 10 mL beaker and heated to about 300 ℃ using a heating mantle. Subsequently, FA gradually carbonized, the solid color gradually changed from yellow to black, after 8 min, all turned black. The carbonized solid was rapidly transferred into a beaker containing 20 mL ultrapure water with continuous ultrasounding. After sonicating for 10 min, the product was centrifuged twice at a high speed (12,000 rpm) for 15 min to remove the solid precipitate. Then, the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm microporous membrane and repeated two more times. The filtrate was then subjected to dialysis against pure water through a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff = 500 Da) for 8 h to obtain pure N-CDs. Furthermore, the pure N-CDs liquid was vacuum lyophilized to give solid N-CDs. Finally, the solid N-CDs were redispersed into ultrapure water as stock solution (200 μg mL−1) and stored in darkness at 4 ℃ for further characterization and use.

The MnO2 solids were prepared according to previously described methods with slight modifications [30]. Briefly, ethanol (99%, 3.3 mL), sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS) (0.5 M, 2.5 mL), and H2SO4 (0.1 M, 0.2 mL) were mixed into 22 mL ultrapure water. The solution was heated to 90 ℃. Then, KMnO4 solution (0.05 M, 2 mL) was slowly dropped to the mixed solution and keeped strring for 30 min to achieve red brown solution. After being cooled to room temperature, the resulting precipitate was separated by centrifugation and purified three times with ultrapure water and ethanol and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min.

The above-prepared MnO2 solids were added to 10 mL 1 mg mL−1 BSA aqueous solution and sonicated for 12 h. Then, the suspension was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 20 min to remove the unexfoliated MnO2. Subsequently, the supernatant was collected and stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃. The concentration of MnO2 nanosheets in the supernatant was calculated by absorption spectra according to Lambert-Beer’s Law with the molar extinction coefficient of 9.6 × 103 M-1 cm-1 at 380 nm [[31]].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above procedure, the sensing solution was formed by mixing MnO2 nanosheets and N-CDs. Concretely, 21 μL of N-CDs aqueous solution (200 μg mL−1) and 6 μL MnO2 nanosheets (8.75 mM) were added into a 0.5 mL calibrated test tube, thoroughly shaken and equilibrated for 1 min until fully mixed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection process for ACP activity
ce:section.ce:para: The ACP assay was carried out as follows: In a typical assay, 42 μL reaction solutions including 21 μL different activities of ACP standard solution (diluted with 10 mM, pH 5.5, Tris−HCl) and 21 μL AAP solution (5 mM, dissolved in 10 mM, pH 5.5, Tris−HCl) were mixed in a 0.5 mL calibrated test tube. Then, these samples were incubated in 37 °C bath for 2 h. After incubation, 27 μL of the prepared N-CDs-MnO2 nanomaterials and 141 μL Tris−HCl (10 mM, pH 5.5) were added into the above solution. The mixtures were incubated in 40 ℃ bath for another 10 min. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of the mixtures was measured at 448 nm with excitation at 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for determining ACP level in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: For real sample assay, drug-free human serum samples of healthy volunteers were obtained from the First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University. All experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines. The serums were diluted 5-fold with Tris−HCl (10 mM, pH 5.5) before analysis. Then, 21 μL AAP solution (5 mM), 10.5 μL diluted serum sample and 10.5 μL different activities of ACP standard solution were mixed in a 0.5 mL calibrated test tube to prepare spiked samples. Then, the experiments were conducted in the same condition as above.

For ACP inhibitor detection, 42 μL reaction solutions including 21 μL AAP solution (5 mM), 10.5 μL ACP standard solution (800 U·L−1) and 10.5 μL different concentration of NaF were mixed in a 0.5 mL calibrated test tube. Then, these samples were reacted and tested according to the detecting conditions of ACP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: To realize the above design, the N-CDs were obtained through FA pyrolysis which is simple and rapid. The morphology of N-CDs was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM image (Fig. 1 A) shows that the monodispersed N-CDs have uniform size with an average diameter about 4.5 nm (Fig. 1B). The result could be well supported by the dynamic light scattering spectrum measurement (Fig. S1A). The high-resolution TEM image (the inset in Fig. 1A) indicates the high crystallinity of the N-CDs, and the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm fit with the (1120) lattice fringe of graphene [32]. In addition, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were conducted to identify the composition and chemical bonding states of N-CDs. In the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1C), the peak at 3382 cm−1 is ascribed to the characteristic absorption of OH stretching vibrations, and the peak of the C–O group conjugated with condensed aromatic carbons appears at 1117 cm−1. Moreover, we can see the characteristic absorption bands of NH stretching vibration at 3298 cm−1, the absorption peak of –C = O (carbonyl) at 1685 cm−1, and the absorption peak of phenyl at 1600 cm−1. And there are the bending vibrations of NH at 1539 cm−1 and OH at 1408 cm−1. Furthermore, two peaks at 1253 cm−1 and 1298 cm−1 are assigned to C–N stretching vibrations and the aromatic CN heterocycles stretching vibrations, respectively [33]. As shown in Fig. 1D, the full XPS spectra of the N-CDs exhibits three predominant peaks at 284.75, 399.25 and 532.45 eV which are attributed to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. S1B) can be well deconvoluted into three surface components, corresponding to sp2 (CC/C-C) at binding energy of 284.5 eV, C–N at 285.3 eV and CO at 287.9 eV [34]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. S1C) exhibits two peaks at 398.9 eV and 399.8 eV, related to C–N and NH [35]. As can be seen from the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S1D), the two fitted peaks at 531.4 and 532.6 eV are ascribed to CO and C–O, respectively [36]. The surface components of the N-CDs determined by XPS are consistent with the characterization results by FT-IR. Additionally, the average zeta potential (ζ-potential) of the N-CDs is about 6.5 mV (Fig. S2A).

The UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra are also studied to investigate the optical properties of the N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1E, N-CDs exhibit two absorption peaks at 268 and 342 nm, which correspond to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and n-π* transition of CO, respectively [33, 34]. The N-CDs show strong blue photoluminescence in water under 365 nm UV light (the inset in Fig. 1E), and exhibit fluorescence emission band at 448 nm when excited with 360 nm. The corresponding fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths in Fig. 1F show that the peaks of emission spectra are nearly not shifted as the change of the excitation wavelength, which suggests good monodispersion of N-CDs in size and fluorescence. Moreover, the QY of the N-CDs is calculated to be 48.20% using quinine sulfate as reference and the detail presents in Supplementary Information (Fig. S3). And as shown in Fig. S4, the N-CDs also possess high photostability under continuous UV irradiation.

As energy acceptor, MnO2 nanosheets are synthesized by KMnO4 reduction. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the UV–vis absorption spectra of MnO2 nanosheets exhibits a broad absorption spectrum from 250 to 600 nm, which consistents with the previously reported optical characteristic of MnO2 nanomaterials [37, 38]. The broad absorption is adaptable to diverse fluorophores emitting at different regions and thus a promising energy acceptor [39]. In order to further understanding the morphology of MnO2 nanosheets, as displayed in Fig. 2B, the MnO2 nanosheets with occasional folds and crinkles have been observed with TEM, which exhibit a typical 2D morphology. Furthermore, the ζ-potential of the MnO2 nanosheets is -31.6 ± 0.4 mV, showing the negative charge of the nanosheets (Fig. S2B). Interestingly, unlike normal MnO2 nanosheets, the as-prepared MnO2 nanosheets are stable in a weakly acid environment. We found that normal MnO2 nanosheets easily aggregated in pH 5.5 Tris−HCl buffer solution, however, the introduction of BSA could greatly improve their stability (Fig. S5). The N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites prepared by normal MnO2 nanosheets are also unstable (the curve “a” in Fig. S9D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing principle of ACP based on N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of this proposed sensing system for ACP detection, the changeable fluorescence intensity of each step is first recorded by fluorescence spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, compared with that of N-CDs, the fluorescence of N-CDs + MnO2 nanocomposites is quenched thoroughly. Correspondingly, the fluorescence of N-CDs can be greatly restored with the addition of AAP + ACP reactants, while a single AAP or ACP has no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs + MnO2 nanocomposites. The inset shows corresponding photographs of each solution taken under visible light (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom). Therefore, the designed nanosensor can be used for ACP detection based on the fluorescence recovery of N-CDs.

Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectra of each step were measured to investigate the interaction of N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets in the composites. As shown in Fig. 3B, the N-CDs exhibit two distinct absorption bands at 268 and 342 nm. The MnO2 nanosheets have intense and broad UV–vis absorption spectrum in the range of 250–600 nm. Due to the electrostatic interactions between NCD and MnO2 nanosheets, N-CDs can easily adhere to the surface of MnO2 nanosheets. Compared with the MnO2 nanosheets, N-CDs + MnO2 nanocomposites have displayed similar UV–vis spectrum whereas with a little blue shift, suggesting the combined absorption properties of N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets [29, 40]. More importantly, the absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets overlaps well with the fluorescence emissions spectrum of the N-CDs (Fig. S6), which results in the efficient FRET between N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets [29]. However, when AAP + ACP reactants were added into the N-CDs + MnO2 sensing system, the absorption at 300–600 nm was reduced, indicating the addition of AAP + ACP reactants can effectively reduce the quantity of MnO2 nanosheets, which may lead to the disappearance of FRET.

In order to study the FRET between N-CDs and MnO2 nanosheets, the quenching efficiency of the nanosensor was studied with different concentrations of MnO2 nanosheets. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs (20 μg mL−1) decreased significantly with the gradual addition of MnO2 nanosheets, and the quenching efficiency of MnO2 nanosheets reached 91.43% when the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets is up to 250 μM. Then, the fluorescence intensity changed slightly with further increasing concentration of MnO2 nanosheets. Fig. 4B displays corresponding photographs of solution taken under visible light (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom). Thus, 20 μg mL-1 of the N-CDs and 250 μM of MnO2 nanosheets were selected for the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence recovery of N-CDs by AA
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the sensing system for the sensitive quantification of AA, the fluorescence responses induced by AA at different concentrations were evaluated. Fig. S7 shows the linear response range and the detection limit of the sensing system under the optimal conditions. Fig. 5 A shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the system upon the addition of different concentrations of AA and the inset clearly represents the fluorescence enhancement factors [(F-F0)/F0] induced by AA increased upon increasing the concentration of AA up to 260 μM. Fig. 5B shows [(F-F0) /F0] is linearly related to the concentration of AA in the range of 30 μM to 220 μM, the regression equation is (F-F0)/F0 = 0.04858[AA]-0.79422 with a correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.9976, and the limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 0.36 μM. Compared with the developed fluorescent probe with other methods reported previously, our present method offered a comparable LOD and a broader dynamic range (Table S1). In addition, the selectivity and corresponding practicability of the nanosensor for AA detection were also studied in Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ACP activity determination based on N-CDs-MnO2 nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal detecting conditions of ACP are presented in Supplementary Information (Fig. S9). Under the optimum conditions, the sensing system was used to detect the activity of ACP. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites increased steadily with the increase of ACP activity from 0.1 to 40 U L−1 (Fig. 6 A). Correspondingly, a good linear relationship between the fluorescence enhancement factors [(F-F0)/F0] and the ACP activity in the range from 5 to 40 U L−1 was plotted in Fig. 6B. The regression equation is (F0-F)/F0 = 0.24637[ACP]-0.42934. The corresponding regression coefficient (R2) is 0.996, and the LOD is calculated to be 0.1 U L−1. The detection limit is defined by the equation LOD = 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 11) and s is the slope of the calibration curve. It has been reported that normal level of ACP in blood serum is 35–123 U L-1 [41]. The sensitivity of this nanoprobe to ACP is extremely excellent with the LOD as low as 0.1 U·L−1, which is about 2 orders lower than the lowest concentration in blood serum. In comparison with other methods as shown in Table S4, the proposed method in our paper is relatively simple, facile and sensitive.

To validate the selectivity of the N-CDs-MnO2 nanoprobe for ACP, the control experiments were conducted with ALP, Try, Ure, Lys, TYR and GOx. Fig. 6C shows the fluorescence enhancement factors [(F-F0)/F0] toward the different targets with the activity of 20 U L−1. We found that the addition of other enzymes into the sensing system did not result in a remarkable change in the fluorescence intensity, whereas upon the addition of ACP, the fluorescence intensity significantly changed. Furthermore, common ions and small molecules also did not produce an obvious signal (Fig. S10). These results demonstrate that the sensing system has excellent selectivity and specificity for ACP detection.

To demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed method in real samples detection, we used it to detect ACP in diluted human serum (1%). As shown in Table 1 , the N-CDs-MnO2 nanoprobe was applied for ACP sensing in three different human serum samples by the standard addition method. The recoveries of the three spiked samples ranged from 97.79% to 103.62%, and the RSDs were no more than 3.74%, indicating the potential applicability of the proposed method to practical analysis of ACP in biological samples.

It is to consider that the AAP hydrolyzed by ACP could be affected when an inhibitor of ACP was introduced. Therefore, the N-CDs-MnO2 nanoprobe could also be used for screening potential ACP inhibitors. In this work, NaF, a common ACP inhibitor, was employed for inhibition. With the addition of NaF to the assay solution, ACP activity could be inhibited, and emission intensity would reduce as expected. We use the inhibition efficiency (IE%) of ACP as a signal to detect of NaF. IE% was expressed by the following equation:
Inhibition Efficiency ( IE ) =
Fno inhibitor − Finhibitor
Fno inhibitor − F0
× 100 %
Where Fno inhibitor and Finhibitor are the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs + MnO2+AAP + ACP system in the absence and presence of NaF, respectively. F0 stands for the initial fluorescence intensity of the proposed system in the absence of ACP and NaF. As shown in Fig. 6D, the inhibition efficiency increased with the increase of NaF concentration, which indicates that the rate of AAP hydrolyzed by ACP was reduced. This result significantly demonstrated that our method could be used for the screening of potential ACP inhibitors.

In summary, N-CDs with high QY were synthesized by a simple method, and then employed as the energy donor to further construct an effective MnO2-regulated redox-active fluorescent sensing platform for highly selective and sensitive monitoring of ACP activity. The MnO2 nanosheets that quenched the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs through FRET can be selectively reduced to Mn2+ ions by AA, which is the hydrolysis product of AAP catalyzed by ACP. Utilizing the variation of fluorescence intensity, a facile and convenient fluorescent “Switch-On” nanosensor for ACP detection was developed with the detection limits of 0.1 U·L−1. Moreover, the presented method was applied to determine the ACP level in human serum samples with satisfactory results. Furthermore, it can be extended for screening inhibitors of ACP. Given its preparation-simple, cost-effective and environmental-friendly, this method has a great potential application in disease diagnosis and evaluation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775023 and 21705021), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2015J01595), Medical Elite Cultivation Program of Fujian, P.R.C. (2014-ZQN-JC-23), the Scientific Research Major Program of Fujian Medical University (JS15004), Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (2016Y9055).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.08.011.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Continuous and sensitive acid phosphatase assay based on a conjugated polyelectrolyte
Graphene quantum dots as selective fluorescence sensor for the detection of ascorbic acid and acid phosphatase via Cr (VI)/Cr (III)-modulated redox reaction
J. Mater. Chem. B Mater. Biol. Med.
A convenient and label-free fluorescence “turn off-on” nanosensor with high sensitivity and selectivity for acid phosphatase
Enzymatic assay of acid phosphatase and microanalysis of Cu2+ and Ag+ with a SAW-impedance sensor
Clinical significance of the human acid phosphatases: a review
High acid phosphatase level indicating Gaucher’s disease in patient with prostatism
Tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase isoform 5b: a novel serum marker for monitoring bone disease in multiple myeloma
Prognostic role of serum prostatic acid phosphatase for 103Pd-based radiation for prostatic carcinoma
Int. J. Radiat. Oncol. Biol. Phys.
Near-infrared fluorescence probe for the determination of acid phosphatase and imaging of prostate cancer cells
Organelle-Specific Detection of Phosphatase Activities with Two-Photon Fluorogenic Probes in Cells and Tissues
Prostatic acid phosphatase is expressed in human prostate cancer bone metastases and promotes osteoblast differentiation
High performance liquid chromatography equipped with a cathodic detector and column-switching device as a high-throughput method for a phosphatase assay with p-nitrophenyl phosphate
Electroanalytical determination of acid phosphatase activity by monitoring p-nitrophenol
A novel fluorescent “Turn-Off/Turn-On” system for the detection of acid phosphatase activity
A colorimetric and near-infrared fluorescent probe with high sensitivity and selectivity for acid phosphatase and inhibitor screening
Reversible fluorescent nanoswitch based on carbon quantum dots nanoassembly for real-time acid phosphatase activity monitoring
A green heterogeneous synthesis of N-doped carbon dots and their photoluminescence applications in solid and aqueous states
Chemical redox modulated fluorescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase
Real-time fluorescence assay of alkaline phosphatase in living cells using boron-doped graphene quantum dots as fluorophores
Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots at low temperature for electrochemical sensing trinitrotoluene
A novel label-free electrochemical immunosensor based on functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for carcinoembryonic antigen detection
Fluorescence determination of nitrite in water using prawn-shell derived nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots as fluorophores
Time-resolved Energy Transfer and Conformational Distributions of Biopolymers
A FRET-based carbon dot-MnO2 nanosheet architecture for glutathione sensing in human whole blood samples
Break-up of two-dimensional MnO2 nanosheets promotes ultrasensitive ph-triggered theranostics of cancer
A rapid fluorescence “switch-on” assay for glutathione detection by using carbon dots-MnO2 nanocomposites
Graphene quantum Dot-MnO2 nanosheet based optical sensing platform: a sensitive fluorescence “Turn Off-On” nanosensor for glutathione detection and intracellular imaging
Room-temperature synthesis of manganese oxide monosheets
Graphene quantum dots as universal fluorophores and their use in revealing regulated trafficking of insulin receptors in adipocytes
A non-redox modulated fluorescence strategy for sensitive and selective ascorbic acid detection with highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles via solid-state synthesis
On-Off-On Fluorescent Carbon Dot Nanosensor for Recognition of Chromium(VI) and Ascorbic Acid Based on the Inner Filter Effect
Green and fast synthesis of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots with deep blue photoluminescence
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
MnO2 nanosheets as an artificial enzyme to mimic oxidase for rapid and sensitive detection of glutathione
“Switch-On” fluorescent sensing of ascorbic acid in food samples based on carbon quantum Dots-MnO2 probe
An MnO2 nanosheet as a label-free nanoplatform for homogeneous biosensing
Signal-on fluorescent sensor based on GQDs-MnO2 composite for glutathione
A simple-potentiometric method for determination of acid and alkaline phosphatase enzymes in biological fluids and dairy products using a nitrophenylphosphate plastic membrane sensor

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhenmao Zhu received his BS degree in Department of Chemistry from Quanzhou Normal University in 2015. He is currently an MS graduate student in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. His research interests are pharmaceutical analysis and fluorescence analysis.

Xiaoyun Lin received her BS degree in Pharmacy from Fujian Medical University in 2016. She is currently an MS graduate student in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. Her research interests are nanosensor development and fluorescence analysis.

Lina Wu received her BS degree in Pharmacy from Fujian Medical University in 2017. She is currently an MS graduate student in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. Her research interests are nanoclusters preparation and nanomedicine analysis.

Chengfei Zhao received his MS degree in Pharmacy from Fujian Medical University in 2014. He is currently a PhD graduate student in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. His research interest is nanomedicine analysis and molecular biology researches.

Yanjie Zheng is an associate professor of Analytical Chemistry in Fujian Medical University. She acquired her PhD degree in Pharmacology from Fujian Medical University in 2016. Her current interests include the biosensors development and pharmaceutical analysis.

Ailin Liu is professor of Analytical Chemistry in Fujian Medical University. He received his PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from Nanjing University in 2006, China. His current interests include the development of Microfluidics analysis and biosensors.

Liqing Lin is a professor of Analytical Chemistry in Fujian Medical University. She acquired her PhD degree in Pharmacology from Fujian Medical University in 2012. Her current interests include the development of biosensors and molecular biology research.

Xinhua Lin is a Professor of Analytical Chemistry in Fujian Medical University. He received his BS and MS degrees in Analytical Chemistry from Xiamen University, China, in 1983 and 1989, respectively. He is also a doctoral supervisor. His research interest is biosensor.
